query.text: I am the end user. Please answer me directly and do not output any internal audit, replay, handoff, log, or internal_capsule content. Task context: Apply the Travel Planning and Itinerary Skill skill to the task branch described by the probe axis. Probe axis: confirm the destination, date range, trip length, traveler count, budget level, and any must-see or must-avoid requirements. Keep the ordering easy to follow..

metadata.scenario: confirm the destination, date range, trip length, traveler count, budget level, and any must-see or must-avoid requirements. Keep the ordering easy to follow.
metadata.probe_axis_text: confirm the destination, date range, trip length, traveler count, budget level, and any must-see or must-avoid requirements. Keep the ordering easy to follow.

output:
Summary: You want me to confirm the trip basics so I can plan: destination, date range, trip length, number of travelers, budget level, and any must-see or must-avoid items. I’ll treat this as the primary intake step for a travel plan.
Action: Please provide the following items in this exact order so I can proceed:
1) Destination (city and country; note if multi-city)  
2) Date range (start date and end date, or arrival and departure dates)  
3) Trip length in nights/days (if different from dates)  
4) Traveler count and profile (adults/children, ages, any mobility or medical needs)  
5) Budget level (tight/economy, mid-range, or high/ luxury; give an approximate total per person if you can)  
6) Must-see priorities (1–3 non-negotiable sights or experiences)  
7) Must-avoid constraints (anything to avoid: stairs, long walks, late nights, specific foods, neighborhoods, etc.)
Caution: I cannot finalize bookings or assert up-to-date visa, opening-hour, or transport details without current data; those require live checks. Also, if any dates are flexible, that affects cost and pacing recommendations—please flag flexibility. If any critical information is missing, I’ll only ask the minimum additional detail needed.
Next Step: Reply with the seven items requested in the same numbered order. Once you do, I’ll confirm the input, state the planning posture, and propose the first concrete deliverable (itinerary skeleton or booking priority).

query.text: I am the end user. Please answer me directly and do not output any internal audit, replay, handoff, log, or internal_capsule content. Probe axis: classify the request across itinerary design, destination research, budget planning, packing support, or risk-aware travel adjustment. Keep the ordering easy to follow..

metadata.scenario: classify the request across itinerary design, destination research, budget planning, packing support, or risk-aware travel adjustment. Keep the ordering easy to follow.
metadata.probe_axis_text: classify the request across itinerary design, destination research, budget planning, packing support, or risk-aware travel adjustment. Keep the ordering easy to follow.

output:
Summary: You asked me to classify your travel request across the planning categories (itinerary design, destination research, budget planning, packing support, risk-aware travel adjustment) so we can proceed with the appropriate planning path.
Action: I will treat this as a classification step to determine which planning track(s) apply. For any real request I’ll map the needs into one or more of these categories and explain the immediate implications:
- Itinerary design — needed when you want day-by-day plans, transport logistics, or pacing trade-offs resolved.
- Destination research — needed when key facts (entry rules, seasonality, local conditions, opening hours) or destination choices are uncertain.
- Budget planning — needed when you require per-person cost estimates, budgeting by category (flights, lodging, activities), or advice to align choices to a stated budget.
- Packing support — needed when you want a tailored packing checklist (climate, activities, carry-on vs checked luggage, restricted items).
- Risk-aware travel adjustment — needed when weather, health, safety, strikes, or other disruptions require contingency plans and flexible bookings.
Caution: This classification is a diagnostic step; it doesn’t replace up-to-date factual checks. I must not fabricate current visa rules, safety conditions, opening hours, or live transport details. If your needs depend on those facts, I’ll request the minimum verified items (dates, destination, traveler count, budget, must-see/must-avoid) before giving detailed scheduling, cost numbers, or safety guidance.
Next Step: Tell me your immediate planning goal and any minimum trip facts you already have (destination, travel dates or date range, trip length, traveler count, budget range, and one must-see or must-avoid). I’ll classify which planning tracks are required and recommend the single next planning action (e.g., build an itinerary skeleton, run destination checks, produce a budget estimate, create a packing checklist, or draft contingency options).

query.text: I am the end user. Please answer me directly and do not output any internal audit, replay, handoff, log, or internal_capsule content. Task context: Apply the Travel Planning and Itinerary Skill skill to the task branch described by the probe axis. Probe axis: state the current travel-planning posture first, then explain the main trade-offs around pacing, logistics, and budget. Keep the ordering easy to follow..

metadata.scenario: state the current travel-planning posture first, then explain the main trade-offs around pacing, logistics, and budget. Keep the ordering easy to follow.
metadata.probe_axis_text: state the current travel-planning posture first, then explain the main trade-offs around pacing, logistics, and budget. Keep the ordering easy to follow.

output:
Summary: Current travel-planning posture: initial assessment stage — we have a travel intent but no confirmed destination, dates, traveler count, budget, or specific must-see/avoid items. At this posture we are clarifying constraints and trade-offs before producing a feasible itinerary.
Action: 1) Identify missing fundamentals quickly: destination, travel dates (or date range), trip length, number of travelers, traveler profiles (ages, mobility, dietary needs), and a target budget band (economy/low, mid-range, or high). 2) Once those are provided, I will produce a paced plan that balances daily activity load, transit time, rest/recovery, and booking priorities (flights, overnight lodgings, major timed attractions). 3) I will flag immediately any items that need up-to-date verification (visas, entry rules, opening hours, local strikes/weather alerts) and recommend what to check and when.
Caution: Key trade-offs to understand now:
- Pacing vs. Coverage: Trying to fit many “must-see” items into limited days raises transit time and fatigue; expect either reduced sightseeing depth or extra travel days. Avoid over-packed days that require early mornings and long transfers unless travelers accept fatigue.
- Logistics vs. Budget: Faster transfers (domestic flights, private drivers) save time but increase cost; slower options (trains, buses) are cheaper but need more buffer time. Accommodation location is critical — cheaper suburbs increase daily transport time and can negate savings.
- Certainty vs. Accuracy: I cannot and will not fabricate time-sensitive facts (visa rules, opening hours, transport schedules). Those require live checks before booking; plan skeletons are safe, exact tickets or timed tours must wait for confirmation.
- Special needs impact choices: Dietary, accessibility, or family constraints may dictate accommodation, dining options, and activity feasibility; these should be confirmed early to avoid last-minute changes.
Next Step: Please provide the minimum required details so I can create a realistic next-stage plan:
- Destination city/country
- Travel date range or exact dates
- Trip length (number of nights)
- Number of travelers and any special needs (ages, mobility, diet)
- Budget level (economy/low, mid-range, high)
- Top 2–3 must-see priorities or must-avoid constraints

With that information I’ll produce a staged plan showing pacing, transport windows, booking priorities, and a clear next checkpoint for live verifications.

To generate a usable itinerary skeleton or booking priority I need (at most) these items: destination city/country, exact or approximate travel dates (arrival and departure), number of travelers and traveler types (adult/child), and your total trip budget or budget category (economy/low, mid-range, or high). If you already have any must-see or must-avoid items (e.g., a site you must visit, mobility limits, dietary needs, or already-booked travel legs), include just those that are fixed.

If any of those are critical to your next decision (e.g., visa needed before booking, festival dates, or a key attraction with limited entry), I will ask a single targeted follow-up rather than multiple checks.

Once you reply with those, I’ll produce a practical next action (itinerary skeleton, booking priorities, or research checklist).

Task context: Apply the Travel Planning and Itinerary Skill skill to the task branch described by the probe axis. Probe axis: When the traveler has ambitious sightseeing goals but limited time, forcing a hard pacing trade-off, acknowledge the complication explicitly, explain how it changes the decision path, and keep the next action narrow and realistic..

metadata.scenario: When the traveler has ambitious sightseeing goals but limited time, forcing a hard pacing trade-off, acknowledge the complication explicitly, explain how it changes the decision path, and keep the next action narrow and realistic.
metadata.probe_axis_text: When the traveler has ambitious sightseeing goals but limited time, forcing a hard pacing trade-off, acknowledge the complication explicitly, explain how it changes the decision path, and keep the next action narrow and realistic.

output:
Summary: You want an actionable plan for a trip where sightseeing goals are ambitious but available time is limited; this creates a hard pacing trade-off between fitting many highlights and allowing time for transport, rest, and realistic on-the-ground delays.
Action: Treat the request as an itinerary design with constrained time. Prioritize must-see items into a ranked list (non-negotiable, desirable, optional), cluster attractions by proximity and travel mode, and build a skeleton day-by-day with no more than 1–2 major highlights plus one light activity per half-day. Reserve explicit time blocks for transit, meals, and recovery. Recommend booking flexible tickets for the top 1–2 attractions per day and keeping others as low-effort backups.
Caution: Key limitation: without exact dates, arrival/departure times, number of travelers, mobility or accessibility needs, and which sights are truly non-negotiable, any daily schedule will be an approximation. Also avoid assuming local transport speeds, opening hours, or entry rules—those must be checked before booking. Over-ambitious plans often lead to rushed days, missed reservations, or fatigue; accept trade-offs (fewer sites done well vs. many done superficially).
Next Step: Provide minimum missing details so I can produce a realistic skeleton: destination city/cities, exact travel dates and arrival/departure times, traveler count and mobility needs, and a ranked list of 3–6 must-see vs. may-see sights. With that I’ll produce a paced day-by-day skeleton with booking priorities and time buffers.

metadata.probe_axis_text: When weather, seasonality, or regional disruptions may materially change the feasibility of the requested itinerary, acknowledge the complication explicitly, explain how it changes the decision path, and keep the next action narrow and realistic.

output:
Summary: You asked for travel-planning guidance that explicitly accounts for weather, seasonality, or regional disruptions which may change itinerary feasibility. The immediate goal is a realistic planning posture that identifies which parts of an itinerary are weather-sensitive and what narrow, practical adjustments are needed.
Action: Treat the plan as condition-dependent. Identify the trip dates, destination(s), and any fixed bookings; flag weather-sensitive items (outdoor attractions, ferry or mountain transfers, scenic drives, seasonal closures). For each flagged item, create one realistic backup: an indoor alternative nearby, a rescheduled time window, or a cancellation with refundable/transferable booking. Prioritize flexible bookings for transport and accommodation (free cancellation or travel insurance covering weather disruptions) and allow buffer days or half-days for contingency.
Caution: I cannot supply or assume current weather forecasts, advisories, opening-hours, transport disruptions, or official local restrictions—those are time-sensitive facts you must check with up-to-date sources (weather services, transit operators, attraction websites, and local authorities). Also avoid tightly packed days relying on single weather-dependent bookings; those create cascade failures if disrupted.
Next Step: Tell me the destination(s) and exact travel dates, plus any non‑flexible bookings or must-see, weather-dependent items (e.g., glacier hike, ferry to islands, high-altitude pass). I will then mark the weather-sensitive elements, propose one-for-one backups, and recommend which bookings to make flexible or insure first.

The immediate goal is to plan or advise in a way that preserves accuracy limits and produces a handoff-ready plan without inventing time-sensitive data.
Action: I will proceed by (1) confirming the minimum trip facts I need, (2) classifying this request as destination research and itinerary planning with a strict evidence requirement, (3) outlining planning trade-offs and what can be safely recommended without live checks, and (4) proposing a concrete next-step research action you or I should take that uses authoritative sources. This keeps the plan usable to another planner and avoids fabricating time-sensitive details.
Caution: I cannot provide authoritative, current visa requirements, local safety advisories, attraction opening hours, or real-time transport schedules. Any specific booking or risk decision that depends on those facts must be verified against official government sites, the attraction or carrier directly, or a live travel-advice source before committing. Recommendations I give will therefore be framed as suggestions, assumptions to validate, or placeholders for you to confirm.

- Trip length and number of travelers
- Budget level (economy / mid-range / premium)
- Any must-see or must-avoid items, health/accessibility/diet constraints

With that, I will return: (A) an itinerary skeleton with realistic pacing and assumptions clearly labeled, (B) a prioritized list of facts you must verify (visa, safety, opening hours, transport, bookings) with recommended authoritative sources, and (C) booking priorities and a one-week timeline for confirmations.

output:
Summary: You want travel planning advice that explicitly avoids unrealistic daily pacing and accounts for travel time, fatigue, weather, and booking constraints so the plan is practical and handoff-ready.
Action: I will treat pacing, transit time, rest, and booking windows as first-order inputs when designing any itinerary. For a typical day I use a simple rule set: limit major activities to 2–3 per full day (or 1–2 for heavy travel days), allocate realistic transit windows between locations (local transfer times + 30–60 minutes buffer), reserve at least one 1–2 hour midday rest or flexible window, and keep evening time unscheduled for meals or delays. When recommending activities I note fixed-time constraints (reservations, opening hours, tours) and flag which items must be prebooked versus flexible.
Caution: I cannot invent up-to-date opening hours, attraction capacities, transport timetables, or weather forecasts — those must be checked and, where required, booked before finalizing a tight schedule. If key travel legs (flights, long-distance trains/ferries) are uncertain, the pacing must stay conservative: allow minimum 3 hours for international arrival procedures, 1.5–2 hours for domestic transfers, and extra buffer for high-traffic seasons or adverse weather.
Next Step: Tell me the destination(s), travel dates (or date range), number of travelers, trip length, traveler profile (ages, mobility), and 2–3 must-see priorities or fixed bookings you already have. I’ll produce an itinerary skeleton with realistic day-by-day pacing, transit buffers, and explicit booking priorities.

Summary: You asked me to classify the travel request into one of five planning categories so we can pick the right next planning path: itinerary design, destination research, budget planning, packing support, or risk-aware travel adjustment.
Action: Based on the probe axis, I will evaluate the traveler’s immediate need by checking (in order): stated goal (sightseeing, business, relaxation, etc.), time sensitivity (fixed dates vs. flexible), known constraints (budget, companions, health/diet/accessibility), and current blockers (weather risks, visa questions, or packed bookings). Each classification maps to a focused planning workflow:
- Itinerary design: build day-by-day skeleton, pace trade-offs, booking priorities.
- Destination research: investigate transport hubs, neighborhoods, seasonality, and must-see options.
- Budget planning: allocate major costs (flights, accommodation, transport, activities), suggest ranges and booking order.
- Packing support: create checklist by climate, activities, and restrictions (carry-on vs. checked).
- Risk-aware travel adjustment: create contingency plans, flexible bookings, indoor alternatives, and safety/health checks.
Caution: This classification depends on minimal trip facts (destination, dates or flexibility, traveler count, budget range, and any must-have/must-avoid items). I cannot reliably pick the correct planning track or produce concrete outputs until those essentials are confirmed. Also, I won’t fabricate current entry rules, local safety status, or opening hours—those require up-to-date checks before final bookings.
Next Step: Tell me the destination, travel dates (or whether dates are flexible), number of travelers, rough budget level (economy/low, mid-range, or high), and one primary trip priority (e.g., must-see sites, food, rest/relaxation, business). I’ll classify the request and start the appropriate planning workflow.

query.text: Give only the final user-visible response for this task and do not include any internal replay or internal_capsule content. metadata.probe_axis_text: state the current travel-planning posture first, then explain the main trade-offs around pacing, logistics, and budget. Keep the ordering easy to follow.

output:
Summary: Current posture: Gathering high-level constraints and preferences before creating an itinerary. I have not yet received destination, dates, traveler count, budget, or must-see/avoid priorities, so planning is in a triage stage focused on assumptions and trade-offs.
Action: I will frame the planning choices around three linked dimensions—pacing, logistics, and budget—and show how different decisions shift the plan:
- Pacing trade-offs: Faster pace (more sites per day, early starts, limited downtime) increases daily transit, fatigue risk, and possible missed experiences; slower pace (fewer activities, longer time at each site) improves enjoyment and recovery but reduces total covered attractions. For short trips prioritize “must-see” items; for longer trips allow buffer days and flexible mornings.
- Logistics trade-offs: Central accommodation reduces daily transit time and cost but may be pricier; splitting bases lets you visit distant regions efficiently but adds luggage moves and check-in overhead. Relying on public transport lowers cost and environmental impact but needs schedule alignment; private transfers increase flexibility and cost.
- Budget trade-offs: A higher budget buys time savings (flights, private transfers), better-location lodging, and pre-booked experiences that avoid queues. A tight budget favors fewer location changes, emphasis on free/low-cost attractions, and advance research to capture low fares. Trade-offs are multiplicative: saving on lodging may increase transit time (affecting pacing) or limit meal options (affecting comfort).
Caution: Key limitation: I cannot finalize bookings or assert up-to-date visa, opening hours, or transport schedules without confirmed dates and destination—those facts change with season and local conditions. Any proposed daily plan must remain provisional until arrival/departure times, lodging location, and must-see priorities are confirmed. Also avoid over-scheduling: plan no more than 2–3 major activities per day for moderate pace and include travel time buffers and at least one half-day for rest or unpredictable delays.
Next Step: Please provide the minimum required details so I can produce a realistic skeleton plan: destination city/countries, travel dates (or date range), trip length, number of travelers, budget level (low / mid / high), and up to three must-see or must-avoid items.

metadata.probe_axis_text: When the traveler has ambitious sightseeing goals but limited time, forcing a hard pacing trade-off, acknowledge the complication explicitly, explain how it changes the decision path, and keep the next action narrow and realistic.

output:
Summary: You have an ambitious list of sights but limited time; that conflict is the central planning constraint. The goal is to prioritize must-see items, balance travel time and recovery, and produce a realistic, paced plan another planner can pick up.
Action: Treat this as a pacing-priority decision. First, identify 1–3 non-negotiable “must-see” items (highest priority). For the remainder, group attractions by proximity and experience type (e.g., museum cluster, outdoor/landscape, neighborhoods). Build a short itinerary skeleton that limits full-day plans to one major area and reserves half-days or restful windows for transit/overnight recovery. Recommend booking any time-sensitive items (tickets, sunrise/sunset viewpoints, intercity trains/flights) before filler activities. If multiple must-sees are far apart, propose either cutting lower-priority items, extending travel days with lower activity density, or shifting to a single-region focus.
Caution: This trade-off requires firm choices: trying to “see it all” will force long transit days, higher cost, and fatigue that reduces enjoyment and increases missed bookings. I cannot fabricate up-to-date opening hours, ticket availability, or transport timetables—those must be checked before final bookings. Also consider weather, seasonal hours, and any mobility or dietary needs that change feasible pacing.
Next Step: Tell me the destination, exact travel dates (or range), total trip length, how many travelers, your top 1–3 non-negotiable must-sees, and your budget level (economy / mid-range / high). With that I’ll produce a realistic 3–7 day skeleton that preserves pacing, booking priorities, and one clear decision (which item to drop or swap) if conflicts remain.

output:
Summary: You’ve asked for travel planning that explicitly accounts for weather, seasonality, or regional disruptions; the planning goal is to produce realistic, resilient options rather than a fixed timetable that may fail if conditions change.
Action: Treat the request as a risk-aware itinerary design. Before finalizing daily plans, identify season-specific hazards (e.g., monsoon, wildfire season, winter storms), likely disruption windows (ferries, mountain passes, local strikes), and high-impact bookings (flights, intercity trains, guided excursions). Prioritize flexible bookings, indoor/alternative activities, and accommodation located to minimize travel time to core activities.
Caution: I cannot assume current local weather advisories, transport strikes, or temporary closures—those are time-sensitive facts that must be checked close to booking/travel. Any recommended schedule or booking priority should therefore be treated as contingent: confirm up-to-date weather forecasts, local transport notices, and attraction opening status before purchasing non-refundable tickets.

output:
Summary: Current travel-planning posture: I have no trip details yet, so I’m in an initial triage posture—ready to shape an itinerary but waiting for destination, dates, trip length, traveler count, budget, and any must-see/avoid constraints. My role is to translate your travel goals into realistic, paced plans that balance logistics and budget.
Action: I will explain the main trade-offs you’ll face so you can choose priorities. Key trade-offs:
- Pacing vs. coverage: More sites per day increases travel time and fatigue; fewer stops lets you enjoy each location, reduces transit stress, and lowers risk of missed connections. Decide whether you prefer a relaxed trip (1–2 major activities/day) or a checklist-style trip (3+ shorter stops/day).
- Logistics vs. flexibility: Central accommodation and fewer long transfers simplify daily plans and lower transit costs; adding multiple bases (or long day trips) expands what you can see but increases time spent packing/moving and the chance of delays. Choose between base-and-explore or multi-city routing.
- Budget vs. experience level: Higher budgets buy convenience (direct transport, centrally located hotels, guided tours) and reduce time waste; tighter budgets often require earlier bookings, more use of public transit, and compromise on location or comfort. Allocate your budget to the priorities that matter most (comfort, experiences, food, transport).
- Booking priority vs. spontaneity: Locking in hard-to-get experiences (museums, special tours, seasonal events) early reduces disappointment but limits last-minute flexibility. If spontaneity is important, keep a few “open” days and prioritize refundable options.
- Risk/timing vs. seasonality: Weather, peak seasons, and local holidays affect feasibility and cost. If your dates fall in high season or known disruption windows, expect higher prices and the need for earlier bookings or alternate plans.
Caution: Without destination and dates I can’t provide up-to-date visa, entry, or transport rules, nor realistic timing for travel legs or booking windows. Also, avoid overloading days—assuming seamless transit and zero fatigue often produces plans that feel good on paper but fail in practice. Any recommendations I give now will be high-level trade-offs until you confirm the missing trip facts.
Next Step: Please confirm the minimum required details: destination city/countries, travel date range (or exact dates), trip length, number of travelers, budget level (tight / mid-range / high), and any must-see or must-avoid items (specific sites, mobility/dietary needs, or non-negotiable constraints). Once I have that, I’ll propose a realistic itinerary skeleton with pacing, booking priorities, and a short checklist of time-sensitive reservations.

Summary: You’ve indicated an ambitious set of sightseeing goals but only a short time available; this creates a hard pacing trade-off between quantity of sites and travel quality (fatigue, transit, and booking feasibility). The immediate goal is to translate those goals into a realistic, recoverable plan that prioritizes must-sees while preserving rest and transit time.
Action: Treat the plan as priority-driven and layered: (1) identify 2–4 non-negotiable must-see items (highest priority), (2) group remaining attractions by geographic proximity and time required, and (3) build a skeleton itinerary that guarantees the must-sees with realistic time buffers for travel and rest. Recommend reserving time for one flexible “overflow” window (half-day) rather than trying to fit every attraction. Emphasize bookings that reduce on-the-day friction (timed-entry tickets, skip-the-line, centrally located lodging).
Caution: Key limitations: without exact dates, arrival/departure times, and which attractions are truly non-negotiable, any detailed day-by-day schedule risks being unrealistic. Over-ambitious daily plans commonly fail due to underestimated transit time, queueing, or traveler fatigue. Also, I can’t confirm opening hours, ticket availability, or current travel restrictions—those must be checked before final bookings.
Next Step: Tell me the destination city(ies), exact travel dates (or arrival/departure times), how many travelers, and list your top 2 non-negotiable must-sees. With that I will produce a realistic 1–3 day itinerary skeleton that fixes the must-sees, shows transit blocks and recovery time, and proposes which bookings to prioritize first (hotels vs timed tickets vs intercity transport).

Summary: You asked for travel planning where weather, seasonality, or regional disruptions could materially affect the itinerary. The immediate goal is to produce a realistic planning posture that acknowledges those risks and gives a narrow, actionable next step so the plan remains feasible.
Action: Treat the trip as risk-aware planning. Identify the travel dates and destination first, then map which days and activities are weather-sensitive (outdoor sightseeing, ferries, mountain passes, festivals). For each sensitive item, create one resilient alternative: an indoor or flexible-time option, a refundable or changeable booking, or a nearby lower-risk day. Prioritize booking essentials with flexible terms (accommodation with free cancellation, changeable transport) and confirm local seasonal patterns (rainy season, heatwaves, monsoon, high winds, winter road closures) from official sources before locking nonrefundable reservations.
Caution: I cannot and will not fabricate up-to-date specifics (exact storm forecasts, current closures, or seasonal advisories). Those details materially change feasibility and must be checked with current local weather services, transport operators, and official tourism or government advisories. Overly ambitious daily plans that ignore travel time, seasonal daylight, or likely disruptions risk wasted bookings or unsafe conditions.
Next Step: Provide the destination, exact travel dates (or date range), and three activities you consider "must-do" (especially any outdoor plans). I will flag which items are weather/season-sensitive, suggest resilient alternatives, and recommend which bookings to make first with flexibility in mind.

output:
Summary: You asked for travel planning guidance that avoids unrealistic daily pacing; the goal is a usable itinerary approach that respects travel time, fatigue, weather, and booking constraints and can be handed off to another planner.
Action: Design the plan around realistic daily windows: allocate travel/transfer time blocks, morning or afternoon sightseeing blocks (not both full-day for long transfers), and at least one mid-day rest or low-effort window per full travel day. Prioritize 2–3 “must-see” items per day only when they are geographically clustered and have reliable opening hours; otherwise reduce to 1–2. Flag any long transfers (over 2 hours) as partial travel days and avoid scheduling tight back-to-back time-sensitive bookings the same day. Build booking priorities in this order: flights/long-distance trains, any timed-entry attractions, accommodation near planned activity clusters, then ground transport and restaurants. For weather-sensitive activities, list indoor alternatives and keep reservations refundable or changeable when possible.
Caution: I cannot confirm exact opening hours, current weather, transport schedules, or visa/safety rules without up-to-date sources—those facts must be checked during booking. Recommendations here assume average mobility and no special medical constraints; if you have accessibility needs, young children, or limited mobility the pacing should be relaxed further. Also, tighter budgets may force trade-offs (e.g., overnight travel to save a day vs. daytime travel with sightseeing loss).
Next Step: Tell me the destination, travel dates (or range), trip length, traveler count and ages, budget level, must-see priorities, and any mobility/diet constraints. With those I’ll produce a handoff-ready itinerary skeleton that explicitly lists travel-time blocks, booking priorities, and alternatives keyed to weather and timing.

metadata.probe_axis_text: state the current travel-planning posture first, then explain the main trade-offs around pacing, logistics, and budget. Keep the ordering easy to follow.

output:
Summary: Current travel-planning posture: we are in an early PLAN-stage posture — gathering traveler constraints and shaping realistic trip structure rather than locking reservations. You’ve asked for guidance on pacing, logistics, and budget trade-offs; I’m treating this as an itinerary-design task that must balance comfort, transport time, and cost.
Action: Explain the main trade-offs and how they change decisions:
- Pacing vs. number of stops: More destinations increases transit time and packing/unpacking overhead. Each additional city typically costs a full travel day for transit and recovery; favor fewer hubs with day trips if you want relaxed days and deeper experiences.
- Logistics (transport mode, location choices) vs. daily flexibility: Choosing trains or driving gives different speed/cost/reliability profiles. Faster air or high-speed rail reduces travel time but raises budget and adds airport/terminal transfer overhead. Central accommodation reduces intra-day transport but may be pricier than outskirts.
- Budget vs. experience level: Mid/low budgets can be preserved by limiting long-distance hops, traveling off-peak within the destination (weekday museums, early/late activities), and booking accommodation with kitchen access. Higher budgets allow premium time-savers (direct flights, private transfers) and better-located stays that compress sightseeing into fewer days.
- Activity intensity vs. recovery/time-of-day: Physically or mentally intense activities (long hikes, full-day tours) need buffer time the day before/after. Scheduling high-energy items in the morning and lighter options in the afternoon helps manage fatigue without extra days.
- Fixed bookings risk vs. flexibility: Pre-paying tours and trains can lower cost and secure spots but reduce ability to adapt to weather or unexpected delays. Keep at least one flexible buffer day or refundable option for critical plans.
- Meal/dietary or accessibility needs vs. location/cost trade-off: If specialized dining or accessibility is required, prioritize accommodation location and confirm transport accessibility; this can push cost up but saves time and reduces stress.
Caution: Key limitations and dependencies: I’m not using real-time data (flights, train schedules, visa or entry rules, current opening hours, or weather) — those must be checked before booking. Exact pacing recommendations change with trip length, arrival/departure times, traveler ages/fitness, and must-see priorities. Avoid over-scheduling: a typical realistic day includes transit, 2–3 main activities, meal breaks, and recovery time; squeezing more risks low enjoyment and missed bookings.
Next Step: Provide the minimum details I need to convert these trade-offs into a concrete skeleton itinerary: destination(s) and arrival/departure dates, number of travelers and ages, overall budget range (low/mid/high or $ amount), mobility/dietary constraints, and 2–3 must-see or must-avoid items. With that I’ll propose a paced 3–7 day skeleton that balances logistics, budget, and recovery.

Caution: I cannot produce reliable booking, visa, safety, or schedule-specific guidance without at least the destination and dates. Any detailed suggestions before those are confirmed risk being unrealistic or unusable — I’ll avoid fabricating time-sensitive facts.
Next Step: Reply with the five items requested: destination, dates, traveler count/constraints, budget level, and up to two must-see or must-avoid priorities. I’ll then return a concise realistic next action (one prioritized planning task and why).

metadata.probe_axis_text: When the traveler has ambitious sightseeing goals but limited time, forcing a hard pacing trade-off, acknowledge the complication explicitly, explain how it changes the decision path, and keep the next action narrow and realistic.

output:
Summary: You have ambitious sightseeing goals but limited time; this creates a hard pacing trade-off between seeing as much as possible and preserving travel time, energy, and realistic logistics. The immediate goal is to convert those high ambitions into a safe, runnable plan that prioritizes must-sees while keeping daily schedules achievable.
Action: Treat the trip as a priority-ranked itinerary. Split requested sights into three tiers: non-negotiable must-sees, high-value alternates, and “nice-to-have” extras. Build a skeleton that assigns at most two major time-consuming items (e.g., museum + neighborhood exploration, or long viewpoint visit + guided tour) per half-day or one full-day intensive outing plus a lighter recovery window. Favor centrally located accommodation and cluster activities geographically to reduce transit time. Reserve one buffer slot per 2–3 days for rest, unexpected delays, or a spontaneous must-do.
Caution: Trying to include every attraction will degrade the experience (rushed visits, missed reservations, fatigue). Constraints such as opening hours, transit time between sites, guided-tour schedules, and local peak traffic can make ambitious lists infeasible. I cannot confirm up-to-date opening hours, reservation availability, or transport times here—those must be checked before final bookings. Also factor in personal limits: jet lag, mobility needs, meal times, and weather can all require further pacing adjustments.
Next Step: Tell me the top 3 non-negotiable must-sees, your travel dates, trip length, number of travelers, and your preferred daily energy level (relaxed / moderate / full-tilt). I’ll produce a prioritized 1–3 day skeleton that keeps daily pacing realistic and shows which items to book first.

metadata.probe_axis_text: When weather, seasonality, or regional disruptions may materially change the feasibility of the requested itinerary, acknowledge the complication explicitly, explain how it changes the decision path, and keep the next action narrow and realistic.

output:
Summary: You want guidance applying the travel-planning skill while explicitly handling weather, seasonality, or regional disruptions that could change an itinerary’s feasibility. The goal is to plan around those risks: acknowledge uncertainty, adjust pacing and activity choices, and define a narrow, realistic next action.
Action: Treat the trip as risk-aware planning. First, identify which parts of the itinerary are weather- or season-sensitive (outdoor excursions, mountain passes, ferry routes, seasonal closures) and which are robust (indoor museums, restaurants, city tours). Prioritize flexible bookings (refundable or changeable transport and accommodation), time buffers between major movements, and backup indoor or nearby alternatives for each weather-sensitive day. For high-risk legs (e.g., ferry-dependent island hops, mountain roads), schedule them earlier where possible and add a contingency day or an alternate route. Use conservative travel-time estimates and avoid back-to-back high-effort days when disruption would cause cascading failures.
Caution: I cannot check up-to-date forecasts, transport advisories, or official closure notices here — those facts change quickly and must be verified with local sources, weather services, and booking providers before finalizing or committing payments. Also, adding contingencies (extra day, refundable tickets) will raise cost or reduce the number of fixed activities possible in the same schedule.
Next Step: Provide the destination, travel dates, and any specific weather-sensitive plans you already booked or consider essential (e.g., a glacier hike, inter-island ferry, national park visit). With that, I’ll mark which legs are highest risk, propose a revised skeleton itinerary with concrete backups, and list the priority bookings to make flexible or refund-protected.

metadata.probe_axis_text: The skill must never fabricate current visa rules, safety conditions, opening hours, or transport details when the request depends on up-to-date facts. Keep the explanation reusable for handoff.

output:
Summary: You asked for travel-planning support with the explicit constraint that I must not fabricate up-to-date facts (visa rules, safety conditions, opening hours, transport details). The immediate goal is to confirm how I’ll proceed under that constraint and what minimum inputs I need to produce a usable plan you or another planner can resume.
Action: I will treat your request as a planning/itinerary task that separates verifiable facts from assumptions. For any item requiring current external data (visa entry rules, up-to-date safety advisories, opening hours, live transport schedules or strike notices), I will either: (a) mark it as “requires verification” and list the exact authoritative sources you or I should check (embassy/consulate, government travel advisories, official attraction sites, airline/rail operator pages), or (b) if you want, fetch live sources yourself and paste them here so I can incorporate them. Meanwhile I will build the planning skeleton that does not depend on those live facts: destination, dates, trip length, traveler count, budget bracket, mobility/diet/accessibility constraints, must-see and must-avoid items, and key booking priorities. That skeleton will include realistic pacing, transfer windows, rest time, and risk-aware alternatives—but any claim about current rules or times will be flagged as needing confirmation.
Caution: I cannot provide definitive statements about visas, current local safety or health advisories, opening hours, or specific transport availability/timetables without checking up-to-date authoritative sources. Any planning decisions that hinge on those items (e.g., whether a traveler needs a visa on arrival, whether a site is open on certain dates, or whether a ferry route is operating) must be verified before booking or finalizing the itinerary. Treat any unverified assumption as provisional.
Next Step: Please provide the minimum details so I can produce a concrete, non-fabricated next step: destination city/country, travel date range (or approximate month), trip length, number of travelers and ages if relevant, budget level (economy/budget, mid-range, premium), and any must-see or must-avoid requirements. If you already have specific items you want verified (visa status, particular attraction opening hours, or booked flights), paste those authoritative links or state that you want me to flag them for you to verify.

metadata.probe_axis_text: The skill must do not suggest unrealistic daily pacing that ignores travel time, fatigue, weather, or booking constraints. Keep the explanation reusable for handoff.

output:
Summary: You asked for travel-planning guidance that avoids unrealistic daily pacing; the goal is a practical, resume-able planning response that balances sightseeing ambition with realistic travel time, fatigue, weather, and booking constraints.
Action: I will treat pacing as a primary constraint when creating or adjusting any itinerary: (1) estimate realistic transit times between major points (including airport transfer and check-in/out), (2) limit major activities per day to a sustainable number (typically 1–2 full-day highlights or 2–3 half-day activities), (3) include buffer windows for rest, meals, weather delays, and booking timing, and (4) flag any single-day plans that require early starts, long transfers, or back-to-back reservations so they can be rebalanced.
Caution: I cannot invent or guarantee up-to-date transport schedules, opening hours, or weather forecasts — those specifics must be checked against current sources before booking. Also, “realistic pacing” depends on traveler profile (age, mobility, jet lag tolerance), exact distances, and time of year; without those details, recommendations will be broadly conservative to avoid over-scheduling.
Next Step: Tell me the destination(s), travel dates (or season), trip length, number of travelers, major must-see priorities, and any mobility/diet/booking constraints. With those I’ll produce a day-by-day skeleton that respects realistic transit times, rest windows, and booking priorities, plus the single next booking you should make first (e.g., internal flights, accommodation, or a timed-ticketed attraction).
